rationale: In the literature review, the citation is not just a list mention: the authors explicitly summarize Chandy et al.'s result that informative appeals work better early and emotional appeals work better later. They use that result to motivate their own expectations about generic (more informative) versus brand (more persuasive) DTCA effectiveness over the product life cycle. Although they do not reuse Chandy et al.'s exact method, they transfer this appeal-timing logic to a different regulated pharmaceutical setting, so the citation plays a meaningful conceptual role.
relation_description: The citing paper applies Chandy et al. (2001)'s appeal-timing insight to frame predictions about when category-level versus brand-level messages should be more effective. It tests that logic in a new context where regulation forces strict separation of generic and brand ad content.
